keywords: course Every topic should lead back to real music: match pitch, hear rhythm, distinguish chord qualities and identify common progressions.

keywords: guide Use theory to answer practical questions: why chords belong together, how progressions move and how a song can be transposed.

description: Turning chords and patterns into something of your own.

title: (Hey) Mr Tambourine Man
description: Bob Dylan wrote "Mr. Tambourine Man" in early 1964, inspired partly by guitarist Bruce Langhorne playing a large tambourine at a recording session. Dylan's own version opened Bringing It All Back Home in March 1965; The Byrds' electric cover, released weeks later, launched the folk-rock movement and gave Dylan his only US No. 1 as a songwriter.

title: (Sugar Pie Honey Bunch) I Can't Help Myself
description: Motown's Holland–Dozier–Holland team wrote "I Can't Help Myself" for the Four Tops in 1965; Lamont Dozier said the "Sugar pie, honey bunch" hook came from memories of his grandfather flirting with his grandmother. It topped the Billboard Hot 100 for two weeks that June.

title: Abracadabra
description: Steve Miller wrote and recorded "Abracadabra" as the title track of his 1982 album. It became the biggest hit of his career, spending two weeks at No. 1 on the Billboard Hot 100.

title: Ain't No Sunshine
description: Bill Withers wrote and recorded "Ain't No Sunshine" for his 1971 debut album Just As I Am, inspired by the film Days of Wine and Roses. The repeated "I know" section was meant as a placeholder for unfinished lyrics, but producer Booker T. Jones and the musicians kept it in the final mix.

title: Amazing Grace
description: A historic hymn whose familiar words and melody developed separately, offering a useful example of how texts, tunes and arrangements can acquire new associations over time.

title: American Pie
description: Don McLean wrote and recorded "American Pie" in 1971. Its extended, allusive lyric traces American music and culture through the 1950s and 60s, opening with the 1959 plane crash that killed Buddy Holly — the event McLean later called "the day the music died."

title: Auld Lang Syne
description: Robert Burns sent "Auld Lang Syne" to the Scots Musical Museum in 1788, saying he took it down from an old man's singing rather than writing it himself. Its familiar tune, adapted from a melody by English composer William Shield, was paired with Burns's words in 1799, and bandleader Guy Lombardo's annual broadcasts later made it a New Year's Eve standard in North America.

title: Bad Moon Rising
description: A compact Creedence Clearwater Revival song from 1969. Its short chord cycle and brisk pulse make it useful for clean changes, confident down-up strumming and complete-song practice.
keywords: Creedence Clearwater Revival Rock / roots rock Three primary chords in common beginner arrangements

title: Blowin' in the Wind
description: Bob Dylan wrote "Blowin' in the Wind" in 1962 and recorded it for his 1963 album The Freewheelin' Bob Dylan. Peter, Paul and Mary's cover, released weeks later, became the bigger hit and helped establish the song as a civil rights and folk-revival anthem.

title: Blue Skies
description: Irving Berlin wrote "Blue Skies" in 1926 as a late addition to the Broadway musical Betsy, reportedly inspired by the birth of his daughter. Belle Baker's opening-night performance drew 24 encores, and though the show closed quickly, the song became one of the biggest hits of 1927.

title: Brown Eyed Girl
description: Van Morrison wrote and recorded "Brown Eyed Girl" in March 1967, capturing the take that made the record on his 22nd attempt at A & R Studios. Despite it becoming his signature song, a contract signed without legal advice meant Morrison received no royalties from it for decades.

title: Bye Bye Love
description: Felice and Boudleaux Bryant wrote "Bye Bye Love", and it was turned down by roughly 30 other acts before The Everly Brothers recorded it in January 1957. It became the duo's first hit, reaching No. 2 on the Billboard pop chart and No. 1 on the country chart.
keywords: The Everly Brothers Rock and roll / country

title: Call Me
description: "Call Me" was composed by Giorgio Moroder as the theme for the 1980 film American Gigolo, with lyrics added by Blondie's Debbie Harry. Released as a single in February 1980, it became one of the year's biggest hits.

title: Can't Have You Around Me Anymore
description: Reneé Rapp released "I Can't Have You Around Me Anymore" on her 2025 album Bite Me through Interscope Records.

title: Counting Stars
description: Ryan Tedder of OneRepublic wrote "Counting Stars", released in 2013 on the album Native. Its music video, filmed in New Orleans, was the first band video in history to reach a billion YouTube views.

title: Creep
description: Thom Yorke wrote "Creep" as a student in the late 1980s, and Radiohead recorded it almost by accident during sessions for their debut album after a producer suggested they try it. A co-writing credit was later added for Albert Hammond and Mike Hazlewood over its melodic resemblance to The Hollies' "The Air That I Breathe."

title: Dirty Old Town
description: Ewan MacColl wrote "Dirty Old Town" in 1949 for the play Landscape with Chimneys, about Salford, the industrial town in Lancashire where he grew up. He recorded it himself in 1952, though the song became far better known through later versions by the Dubliners and the Pogues.

title: Don't Worry be Happy
description: Bobby McFerrin wrote and performed "Don't Worry, Be Happy" entirely a cappella, overdubbing every vocal and percussive sound himself. Released in 1988, it became the first a cappella recording to reach No. 1 on the Billboard Hot 100.

title: Fly Me to the Moon
description: Bart Howard wrote the song in 1954 under the title "In Other Words"; it was first recorded that year by Kaye Ballard. Frank Sinatra's 1964 recording with Count Basie's orchestra popularized the now-familiar title and became closely associated with the Apollo Moon missions.

title: Folsom Prison Blues
description: Johnny Cash wrote and recorded "Folsom Prison Blues" in 1955 at Sun Studio in Memphis. Its melody closely echoed Gordon Jenkins's 1953 "Crescent City Blues", and Cash later paid Jenkins a settlement after a lawsuit over the resemblance.

title: Get Back
description: Paul McCartney wrote "Get Back", credited to Lennon–McCartney, and the Beatles played it during their rooftop concert atop Apple Corps' London headquarters on 30 January 1969 — their final live performance. Released as a single that April, it reached No. 1 in numerous countries.

title: Girls Just Wanna Have Fun
description: Robert Hazard wrote "Girls Just Want to Have Fun" in 1979 from a male perspective. Producer Rick Chertoff brought it to Cyndi Lauper, who reworked it into the feminist pop anthem released in 1983, and its music video passed one billion YouTube views in 2022.

title: Hallelujah
description: Leonard Cohen wrote "Hallelujah" over roughly five years, releasing it on his 1984 album Various Positions. The album was initially rejected by Columbia Records in the US, and the song only became widely known after later cover versions by John Cale and Jeff Buckley.

title: Havana
description: Camila Cabello wrote "Havana" with Pharrell Williams and Ali Tamposi, drawing on her Cuban heritage; she has said it took roughly five months and seven different versions to finish. Released in 2017 as the lead single from her debut solo album, it became her first US No. 1 hit.

title: Have You Ever Seen the Rain
description: John Fogerty wrote "Have You Ever Seen the Rain", released by Creedence Clearwater Revival in December 1970. Fogerty later said the song's contrasting imagery reflected tensions within the band ahead of his brother Tom's departure.
keywords: Creedence Clearwater Revival Rock / roots rock

title: Here Comes the Sun
description: George Harrison wrote and sang "Here Comes the Sun" for the Beatles' 1969 album Abbey Road, recording it at EMI Studios that summer. It is now the most-streamed Beatles song on Spotify.

title: Here There and Everywhere
description: Paul McCartney began writing "Here, There and Everywhere" while waiting by John Lennon's pool in 1966, citing the Beach Boys' "God Only Knows" as an inspiration for its shifting chords. Recorded for Revolver, it became a favorite of both Lennon and McCartney among their own songs.

title: Hey Good Lookin'
description: Hank Williams wrote "Hey, Good Lookin'" in a few minutes during a tour flight in 1951 and recorded it that March at Castle Studio in Nashville. It became his fifth No. 1 country hit, though its title and some phrasing echo Cole Porter's 1942 song of the same name.

title: Hey Jude
description: Paul McCartney wrote "Hey Jude" in 1968 to comfort John Lennon's young son Julian during his parents' divorce. Credited to Lennon–McCartney, it topped the charts in Britain for two weeks and in America for nine.

title: Hotel California
description: Don Felder wrote the music for "Hotel California", with lyrics by Don Henley and Glenn Frey; the Eagles released it as a single in February 1977 from the album of the same name. It won the Grammy for Record of the Year in 1978.

title: House of the Rising Sun
description: "House of the Rising Sun" is an American folk song of uncertain authorship, first collected in Appalachia in the 1930s. The Animals' hard-edged 1964 arrangement, recorded in a single take, topped the charts on both sides of the Atlantic; the arranging credit went to keyboardist Alan Price alone, a lasting source of tension in the band.

title: I Can't Stand the Rain
description: Ann Peebles snapped "I can't stand the rain" while heading out one Memphis evening in 1973, and songwriter Don Bryant turned the phrase into a song that night with Bernie Miller. Producer Willie Mitchell used a newly available electric timbale to create its distinctive raindrop riff, and it became Peebles's biggest hit.

title: I Feel for You
description: Prince wrote "I Feel for You" for his 1979 self-titled album after Patrice Rushen turned it down. Chaka Khan's 1984 cover, featuring a rap by Melle Mel and harmonica from Stevie Wonder, became the far bigger hit and won Prince a Grammy for Best R&B Song.

title: I Think I Like You Better When You're Gone
description: Reneé Rapp released "I Think I Like You Better When You're Gone" on her 2025 album Bite Me.

title: I'll Fly Away
description: Albert E. Brumley began "I'll Fly Away" in 1928 while picking cotton, humming an old ballad about wings of an angel, and took roughly three years to finish it before its 1932 publication. It has since become one of the most recorded gospel songs of all time.

title: Itchycoo Park
description: Steve Marriott and Ronnie Lane wrote "Itchycoo Park" for the Small Faces, recording it at Olympic Studios in early 1967. Engineer George Chkiantz's use of flanging on the track made it one of the first pop hits to feature the effect.

title: Jolene
description: Dolly Parton wrote and released "Jolene" as the title track of her 1974 album, and it became her second solo No. 1 country single. The song's tale of a housewife confronting a rival has since become one of her most covered songs.

title: Kiss
description: Prince wrote and recorded "Kiss" for the 1986 album Parade. Revolution bassist Brownmark says he contributed the second verse but was never given formal credit or royalties.

title: Kiss Me
description: Matt Slocum wrote "Kiss Me" for Sixpence None the Richer, released on their self-titled 1997 album. It became a top-five hit in 1998 after featuring in the film She's All That.

title: Leaving on a Jet Plane
description: John Denver wrote "Leaving on a Jet Plane" (originally titled "Babe I Hate to Go") during a 1966 airport layover and recorded it himself for his 1969 album Rhymes & Reasons. Peter, Paul and Mary's 1969 cover became the more famous version and their only US No. 1 hit.

title: Let It Be
description: Paul McCartney wrote "Let It Be" after dreaming about his late mother during a difficult period in 1968; the Beatles released it as the title track of their final studio album in 1970.

title: Like a Rolling Stone
description: Bob Dylan wrote and recorded "Like a Rolling Stone" in June 1965 at Columbia's Studio A in New York, with Mike Bloomfield on guitar and Al Kooper on organ. Rolling Stone magazine has repeatedly named it the greatest song of all time.

title: Mission Impossible
description: Lalo Schifrin composed the Mission: Impossible theme for the 1966 CBS series, reportedly writing the main riff in about 90 seconds. Its distinctive 5/4 time signature and two Grammy wins made it one of television's most recognizable themes.

title: Move It on Over
description: Hank Williams wrote and recorded "Move It on Over" at his first MGM session on 21 April 1947 in Nashville, backed by Red Foley's band. It became his first major hit and is often cited as an early precursor to rock and roll.

title: Mull of Kintyre
description: Paul McCartney and Denny Laine wrote "Mull of Kintyre" as a tribute to the Scottish peninsula, recording it with Wings at McCartney's High Park Farm in August 1977 with the Campbeltown Pipe Band on bagpipes. It became the 1977 Christmas No. 1 in the UK and Wings' biggest hit there.

title: No Diggity
description: Blackstreet's Teddy Riley, Chauncey Hannibal and William Stewart wrote "No Diggity" with Dr. Dre and Queen Pen, sampling Bill Withers. Released in 1996, it topped the Billboard Hot 100 and won a Grammy for Best R&B Performance by a Duo or Group.

title: No Woman No Cry
description: Though credited to Vincent Ford, a friend of Bob Marley's who ran a soup kitchen in Trenchtown, "No Woman, No Cry" is widely believed to have been written largely by Marley himself. The studio version appeared on Natty Dread (1974), but the best-known recording is the live version from the Lyceum Theatre, London, in 1975.

title: Over the Rainbow
description: Harold Arlen composed the melody for "Over the Rainbow" while driving down Sunset Boulevard, and Yip Harburg wrote the lyrics for The Wizard of Oz (1939), where Judy Garland introduced it. The studio nearly cut the song, fearing it slowed the story, but it won the Academy Award for Best Original Song and became Garland's signature.

title: Paperback Writer
description: Paul McCartney wrote "Paperback Writer" after his aunt challenged him to write about something other than love, drawing the idea from a newspaper article about paperback authors. Recorded with John Lennon's help in April 1966, it topped charts in several countries that summer.

title: Place in the Sun
description: Ronald Miller and Bryan Wells wrote "A Place in the Sun" for Stevie Wonder, who recorded it in 1966 at age 16. Its hopeful, socially conscious lyric marked an early step from his child-star pop hits toward more substantive songwriting, and it reached No. 9 on the Billboard pop chart.

title: Proud Mary
description: John Fogerty wrote "Proud Mary" within an hour of receiving his discharge from the Army Reserve in 1968, saying it was the first song he was ever proud of. Released by Creedence Clearwater Revival in January 1969, it reached No. 2 on the Billboard Hot 100, and Ike & Tina Turner's 1971 cover later won a Grammy.
keywords: Creedence Clearwater Revival Rock / roots rock

title: Rhiannon
description: Stevie Nicks wrote "Rhiannon" around Halloween 1974 after encountering the name in the novel Triad, unaware at the time of the Welsh mythological queen it echoed. Fleetwood Mac released it on their 1975 self-titled album, and Nicks often introduced it live as "a song about an old Welsh witch."

title: Ring of Fire
description: June Carter and Merle Kilgore wrote "Ring of Fire", reportedly inspired by an underlined phrase in an old poetry book; Carter's sister Anita recorded it first in 1963. After hearing that version, Johnny Cash said he dreamed of it with Mexican-style trumpets and recorded his own take, which became one of his biggest hits.

title: Riptide
description: Vance Joy began writing "Riptide" on guitar in 2008 and finished it four years later on ukulele while living with a friend. Released on his 2013 debut EP, its offbeat, image-laden lyric became one of Australia's most enduring pop hits.

title: Row Row Row Your Boat
description: "Row, Row, Row Your Boat" first appeared in print in 1852, though its familiar round-song melody is usually linked to an 1881 arrangement credited to Eliphalet Oram Lyte. Its true authorship remains uncertain, as with many traditional nursery rhymes.

title: Royals
description: Lorde wrote the lyrics to "Royals" in about half an hour in mid-2012, taking the title from a photo of baseball player George Brett signing autographs for the Kansas City Royals. She and producer Joel Little recorded it in a week at Auckland's Golden Age Studios, and it won two Grammy Awards in 2014.

title: Seven Nation Army
description: Jack White came up with the riff for "Seven Nation Army" during a 2002 soundcheck at a Melbourne pub and lowered its pitch with a guitar effects pedal to sound like a bass. Released in 2003 on the White Stripes' album Elephant, its stomping riff became one of the most widely chanted hooks in sports arenas worldwide.

title: Silent Night
description: Joseph Mohr wrote the words to "Silent Night" in 1816 and asked schoolteacher Franz Xaver Gruber to set them to music for guitar in time for Christmas Eve Mass in 1818 at a church in Oberndorf, Austria. Now translated into hundreds of languages, it is one of the most recorded songs in history and was named UNESCO intangible cultural heritage in 2011.

title: Smells Like Teen Spirit
description: Kurt Cobain wrote "Smells Like Teen Spirit" shortly before recording Nevermind in 1991, aiming for what he called "the ultimate pop song" inspired by the Pixies' loud-quiet dynamics. Its title came from a friend's graffiti referencing a deodorant brand, which Cobain misread as a revolutionary slogan.

title: Stairway to Heaven
description: Jimmy Page began composing "Stairway to Heaven" at the remote Welsh cottage Bron-Yr-Aur, with Robert Plant adding lyrics during sessions for Led Zeppelin IV in 1971. Never released as a single, it became one of rock's most played songs regardless.

title: Stand by Me
description: A repeating four-chord soul classic that is ideal for practising an unbroken groove. Its circular progression also makes it a strong first example for hearing harmonic function.
keywords: Ben E. King Soul / doo-wop I–vi–IV–V C, Am, F, G (common ukulele arrangement)

title: Sway
description: Pablo Beltrán Ruiz composed "¿Quién Será?" in 1953, a mambo-bolero later given new English lyrics by Norman Gimbel and popularized as "Sway" by Dean Martin in 1954. Pérez Prado's 1960 instrumental version brought the tune back to its Latin roots for a new audience.

title: Sweet Caroline
description: Neil Diamond wrote "Sweet Caroline" in about an hour in a Memphis hotel room in 1969, needing a three-syllable name for the melody. He has given differing accounts of its inspiration over the years, at one point citing a photo of a young Caroline Kennedy; it remains a fixture at sporting events, especially Boston Red Sox games.

title: Sweet Child O' Mine
description: Guns N' Roses wrote "Sweet Child o' Mine" almost by accident in 1987 after Slash began noodling a "circus" riff during a jam session; Axl Rose set it to a poem he had written for his then-girlfriend Erin Everly. It became the band's only US No. 1 single.

title: Sweet Home Chicago
description: A widely interpreted blues standard associated with Robert Johnson's 1936 recording. On ukulele it becomes a practical route into 12-bar form, shuffle feel and the I–IV–V relationship.
keywords: Robert Johnson Blues I–IV–V within a 12-bar blues form

title: Take Me Home, Country Roads
description: A familiar John Denver song with a repeating chord vocabulary and clear phrases. It is a useful early full-song project for steady strumming, singing and transposition.
keywords: John Denver Country / folk-pop C, F, G, Am (common ukulele arrangement)

title: The Gambler
description: Don Schlitz wrote most of "The Gambler" in his head during a 20-minute walk home in 1976, later crediting the song's final verse to his late father. After two years of pitching it around Nashville, Kenny Rogers's 1978 recording became a country and pop crossover hit and won a Grammy.

title: The Lion Sleeps Tonight
description: South African singer Solomon Linda wrote and recorded "Mbube" with his group the Evening Birds in 1939, the first African record to sell over 100,000 copies. Folk artists including Pete Seeger popularized it as "Wimoweh," and songwriter George David Weiss added English lyrics for the Tokens' 1961 hit "The Lion Sleeps Tonight"; Linda's family only received a fair share of royalties in 2006, decades after his death.

title: The Passenger
description: Guitarist Ricky Gardiner wrote the music for "The Passenger" after being struck by a chord sequence while walking through an orchard, then brought it to Iggy Pop in Berlin. Pop's lyrics, partly inspired by a Jim Morrison poem and by riding Berlin's S-Bahn with David Bowie, were recorded for the 1977 album Lust for Life.

title: The Sound of Silence
description: Paul Simon wrote "The Sound of Silence" at 21, working in a darkened bathroom whose tiled acoustics he liked. The original acoustic version, recorded in 1964, sold poorly until producer Tom Wilson overdubbed electric instruments without the duo's knowledge in 1965, turning it into a No. 1 hit.

title: This Little Light of Mine
description: "This Little Light of Mine" emerged in African American churches in the 1920s; its true author is unknown, though Harry Dixon Loes is often credited for a popular 1940s arrangement he never copyrighted. The song later became an anthem of the civil rights movement.

title: Three Little Birds
description: A Bob Marley & The Wailers song from the 1977 album Exodus. It offers a friendly introduction to relaxed pulse, offbeat accompaniment and choosing a singing key.

title: Time After Time
description: Cyndi Lauper and Rob Hyman wrote "Time After Time" at a piano in early 1983, both going through difficult relationships at the time; Lauper took the title from a listing for the film Time After Time in TV Guide. Released in 1984, it became her only US No. 1 single.

title: Tin Pan Alley
description: Pianist Curtis Jones first recorded a song called "Tin Pan Alley" in 1941, and a related version credited to Bob Geddins was cut by Jimmy Wilson in 1953. Stevie Ray Vaughan's slow-blues 1983 recording, subtitled "Roughest Place in Town," is the version most listeners know today.

title: Walk Like an Egyptian
description: Liam Sternberg got the idea for "Walk Like an Egyptian" watching ferry passengers stagger awkwardly across a choppy English Channel crossing, which reminded him of Egyptian tomb paintings. The Bangles' 1986 recording became their first US No. 1 single and Billboard's top song of 1987.

title: Walking on Sunshine
description: Kimberley Rew wrote "Walking on Sunshine" for Katrina and the Waves' 1983 album of the same name, later saying it was simply "a piece of simple fun" rather than inspired by any single event. A re-recorded version became the band's breakthrough US and UK hit in 1985.
keywords: Katrina and the Waves Pop rock

title: Wonderful Life
description: Colin Vearncombe, performing as Black, wrote "Wonderful Life" in about ten minutes during a period of homelessness and hardship, later saying the title was meant ironically. First released in 1986 on an independent label, a 1987 reissue became a top-ten hit across Europe.

title: Year 3000
description: Busted's James Bourne wrote "Year 3000" partly inspired by his fondness for the film Back to the Future and Robbie Williams's "Millennium". Released in 2003, it reached No. 2 on the UK Singles Chart.

title: Yellow
description: Chris Martin wrote the melody for "Yellow" at Rockfield Studios in Wales while trying to sound like Neil Young, and named it after a phone directory, the Yellow Pages, that happened to be nearby. Coldplay released it in 2000 from their debut album Parachutes, filming its one-shot video on a rainy beach in Dorset.
